headline: Nasdaq Logs Longest Winning Streak Since 2021 as Investors Look Beyond War
summary: Oil prices have retreated.  The prospect of a peace deal that reopens the Strait of Hormuz has now powered the Nasdaq composite to its longest streak of daily gains since November 2021—a 10-session ascent that has lifted the tech-heavy index 14%.  The Dow Jones Industrial Average’s energy-hungry components have the blue-chip index lagging behind other major benchmarks, on Tuesday notching a 0.7% gain.

content.title: Nasdaq Logs Longest Winning Streak Since 2021 as Investors Look Beyond War

headline: How The Five Below (FIVE) Investment Story Is Shifting With New Targets And Expectations
summary: The latest analyst update on Five Below lifts the fair value estimate from US$229.32 to US$261.32, implying a higher long term price target for the shares. That shift lines up with recent Street commentary, where bullish voices are moving targets into the mid to high US$200s, while more cautious analysts still question how much optimism is already priced in. As you read on, you will see how to interpret these changing targets and track the evolving story around growth, execution and...

headline: JPM Q1 Deep Dive: Markets, Asset Management, and Regulatory Uncertainty Shape Outlook
summary: Global financial services giant JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) beat Wall Street’s revenue expectations in Q1 CY2026, with sales up 9.8% year on year to $50.54 billion. Its non-GAAP profit of $5.94 per share was 7.8% above analysts’ consensus estimates.

headline: Bank of America’s Stock Traders Book Record Revenue Amid Volatility
summary: Bank of America’s equities trading business drove record-high revenue in the first quarter as geopolitical tensions and artificial intelligence-related concerns prompted intense stock market volatility.  The second-largest U.S. bank said equities sales and trading revenue of $2.8 billion was a record, up nearly 30% from $2.2 billion a year earlier, on heightened client activity.  Bank of America said its overall sales and trading revenue jumped 13% from a year ago to $6.4 billion, its highest in more than a decade.

headline: Like Other Bank CEOs, BofA's Moynihan Says U.S. Economy Is ‘Resilient’
summary: Bank of America Chief Executive Officer Brian Moynihan said the U.S. economy is “resilient,” joining a chorus of bank CEOs who see similar strength in the face of geopolitical tensions.  “We remain watchful of evolving risks,” Moynihan, who runs the second-largest U.S. lender, said in a statement.  “However, we saw healthy client activity, including solid consumer spending and stable asset quality, indicating a resilient American economy.”

content.title: Like Other Bank CEOs, BofA's Moynihan Says U.S. Economy Is ‘Resilient’
content.summary: Bank of America Chief Executive Officer Brian Moynihan said the U.S. economy is “resilient,” joining a chorus of bank CEOs who see similar strength in the face of geopolitical tensions.  “We remain watchful of evolving risks,” Moynihan, who runs the second-largest U.S. lender, said in a statement.  “However, we saw healthy client activity, including solid consumer spending and stable asset quality, indicating a resilient American economy.”

headline: Hedge Funds Pivot to Bearish Dollar Bets on US-Iran Talks Optimism
summary: (Bloomberg) -- Hedge funds are increasingly downbeat on the dollar as the prospect of renewed US-Iran talks and a possible peace deal sap the currency’s war-driven strength. headline: Copper Prices Swing as Traders Eye Talks to End Iran Conflict
summary: (Bloomberg) -- Copper drifted lower, paring a strong monthly gain as traders eyed the prospect of peace negotiations between the US and Iran.The industrial metal was down 0.3% by late-morning in London, having earlier risen as much as 0.8% to push past a closing price of $13,343.50 a ton on Feb. 27, the day before the US and Israel began strikes on Iran. headline: Wall Street Is Searching for Cockroaches in Private Credit. Here’s What Has Happened So Far.
summary: Private credit was the shiniest thing on Wall Street for years.  Investment firms started lending directly to private companies and others without using banks as middlemen, often at high interest rates.  For many wealthy individuals, the funds were akin to gaining access to the market’s velvet-roped sections, where the best returns tend to be kept for only the biggest names.

content.title: Wall Street Is Searching for Cockroaches in Private Credit. Here’s What Has Happened So Far.
content.summary: Private credit was the shiniest thing on Wall Street for years.  Investment firms started lending directly to private companies and others without using banks as middlemen, often at high interest rates.  For many wealthy individuals, the funds were akin to gaining access to the market’s velvet-roped sections, where the best returns tend to be kept for only the biggest names.

headline: Five Things We Learned From Bank Earnings
summary: The nation’s biggest banks this week painted a reassuring picture of the U.S. economy, saying consumers are still spending despite surging gas prices since the Iran war began.  Trading desks are raking in money from volatile markets and dealmakers are busier than they have been in a while.

headline: Jamie Dimon Says JPMorgan Stock Isn’t Cheap. Why the Bank Is Still Buying Back Billions.
summary: CEO Jamie Dimon says  JPMorgan  Chase’s stock isn’t cheap, but that hasn’t stopped the company from buying back $32 billion of stock over the past four quarters.  The bank has one of the larger share repurchase programs in the country, and uses more than half its annual earnings for stock buybacks.  There’s reason for JPMorgan to rein in its buybacks while the stock trades at lofty multiples—for the benefit of the bank itself and its shareholders.

content.title: Jamie Dimon Says JPMorgan Stock Isn’t Cheap. Why the Bank Is Still Buying Back Billions.
content.summary: CEO Jamie Dimon says  JPMorgan  Chase’s stock isn’t cheap, but that hasn’t stopped the company from buying back $32 billion of stock over the past four quarters.  The bank has one of the larger share repurchase programs in the country, and uses more than half its annual earnings for stock buybacks.  There’s reason for JPMorgan to rein in its buybacks while the stock trades at lofty multiples—for the benefit of the bank itself and its shareholders.

headline: Jamie Dimon Sells $40 Million of JPMorgan Stock
summary: JPMorgan Chief Executive Jamie Dimon sold more than $40 million of the bank’s stock on Wednesday in the latest cash out of his shares.  Dimon has run the bank for over two decades and didn’t start selling any of his bank stocks until the end of 2023 when he sold a million shares worth around $180 million.  Dimon, his wife and his family trusts hold the shares.
